family_history: Mother: systemic lupus erythematosus, managed with hydroxychloroquine
No family history of epilepsy or psychotic disorders

social_history.alcohol: Social drinking on weekends, 3-4 drinks per occasion
social_history.recreational_drugs: Tried marijuana twice in the past, denies current use
social_history.living_situation: Lives in campus dormitory with a roommate
social_history.recent_stressors: Upcoming final exams, recent breakup with boyfriend 3 weeks ago

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert and oriented to person only. Intermittently cooperative but becomes suspicious and guarded when questioned about her symptoms. Speech is fluent but tangential with occasional loose associations. She expresses paranoid ideation, stating that her roommate is 'reading her thoughts' and that 'the cameras are watching.' Affect is labile, alternating between flat and inappropriately tearful. She is unable to perform serial 7s. Recall is 1 out of 3 objects at 5 minutes. She intermittently appears to respond to internal stimuli, turning her head as if hearing voices, though she denies auditory hallucinations when asked directly.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils equal and reactive, 3 mm bilaterally. Extraocular movements intact without nystagmus. Facial sensation intact bilaterally. Facial symmetry preserved. No overt orofacial dyskinesias noted during this examination, though intermittent subtle lip movements observed that could represent mannerisms or early dyskinesias. Hearing grossly intact. Palate elevates symmetrically. Tongue midline without fasciculations.
neurological_exam.motor: Tone normal throughout. Strength 5/5 in all extremities. No drift on pronator testing. No involuntary movements clearly identified during motor examination, though patient is restless and fidgety.
neurological_exam.sensory: Intact to light touch, pinprick, and proprioception in all extremities. No extinction on double simultaneous stimulation.
neurological_exam.reflexes: Biceps 2+ bilaterally, triceps 2+ bilaterally, brachioradialis 2+ bilaterally, patellar 2+ bilaterally, Achilles 2+ bilaterally. Plantar responses flexor bilaterally.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose intact bilaterally without dysmetria. Heel-to-shin intact. Rapid alternating movements slightly slow but likely related to inattention rather than true cerebellar dysfunction.
neurological_exam.gait: Ambulates independently with normal stride length and base. Tandem gait intact. Romberg negative.
neurological_exam.additional: Patient is intermittently agitated, requiring redirection. During one episode of agitation, brief rhythmic lip-smacking movements were noted lasting approximately 5 seconds, which the patient did not recall afterward. This could represent an oral automatism or a stress-related mannerism. No clear autonomic instability during the examination, though resting heart rate is mildly elevated. The patient's pre-existing anxiety disorder and recent psychosocial stressors create a strong pull toward a primary psychiatric diagnosis.

patient.chief_complaint: Bizarre behavior, paranoia, and insomnia for one week
patient.history_present_illness: This 22-year-old right-handed woman was brought to the emergency department by her mother and roommate because of one week of increasingly bizarre behavior, paranoia, and severe insomnia. The roommate reports that approximately ten days ago the patient seemed 'off' — she was unusually quiet, withdrew from social activities, and stopped attending her classes during the final exam period. Her roommate initially attributed this to stress related to her recent breakup and academic pressure. However, over the following days, the patient became increasingly suspicious and paranoid. She accused her roommate of going through her belongings and monitoring her phone. She began sleeping only 1-2 hours per night, pacing the room and talking to herself. Five days ago, she called her mother in tears, speaking rapidly and incoherently about conspiracies involving her professors. Her mother drove three hours to campus and found her daughter disheveled, agitated, and expressing beliefs that people were poisoning her food. The mother took her to the campus health center, where a physician diagnosed an acute anxiety exacerbation and prescribed lorazepam 0.5 mg as needed, which provided only brief periods of sedation. Over the last two days, the patient has refused to eat, stating the food is contaminated. She has become increasingly disorganized in her thinking and was found by her roommate standing motionless in the hallway staring at the wall for several minutes before suddenly becoming combative when touched. The mother reports that the patient has never had psychiatric hospitalizations, has never displayed psychotic symptoms previously, and that her anxiety has been well-controlled on sertraline. There is no history of seizures. The mother denies awareness of any illicit drug use. She notes that her daughter had a mild cold approximately two weeks ago with sore throat and low-grade fever that resolved without treatment. The mother is particularly concerned because her own sister was diagnosed with lupus at a similar age, and she wonders if there could be an autoimmune component, though the emergency physician reassured her that this appears to be a psychiatric presentation.

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 8 Hz alpha rhythm, present but poorly sustained and intermittently attenuated

findings.morphology: Semi-rhythmic medium amplitude theta activity intermixed with background
findings.state: Present during wakefulness, increasing during drowsiness
findings.clinical_correlation: Non-specific finding indicating mild diffuse cerebral dysfunction; may be seen in toxic-metabolic encephalopathy, medication effect, or early encephalitis

findings.morphology: Intermittent runs of rhythmic delta activity lasting 2-5 seconds, moderate amplitude
findings.clinical_correlation: FIRDA is a non-specific finding that can be seen in metabolic encephalopathy, deep midline lesions, or diffuse cortical dysfunction. Not diagnostic of any specific etiology.

artifacts.description: Intermittent frontalis and temporalis muscle artifact during periods of agitation

activating_procedures.hyperventilation: Patient unable to cooperate with hyperventilation
activating_procedures.photic_stimulation: Normal photic driving response at 12-18 Hz. No photoparoxysmal response.

eeg.impression: Mildly abnormal EEG for age showing intermittent generalized theta slowing and brief runs of frontal intermittent rhythmic delta activity (FIRDA). No epileptiform discharges, no electrographic seizures, and no extreme delta brush pattern identified during this 30-minute recording. The findings are non-specific and could be consistent with medication effect, metabolic disturbance, early encephalitis, or a number of other etiologies. Clinical correlation is recommended.
eeg.limitations: Recording limited to 30 minutes in an intermittently uncooperative patient. Brief segments of muscle artifact during agitation may obscure underlying activity. Continuous EEG monitoring may be considered if clinical concern for seizures persists.
recommended_actions: Consider repeat or continuous EEG if clinical status changes
Consider MRI brain to evaluate for structural etiology

findings.location: Two punctate foci in the deep periventricular white matter of the right frontal lobe

additional_observations: Ventricles normal in size and configuration
Medial temporal lobes appear normal bilaterally
Normal appearance of the hippocampi bilaterally
mri.impression: Essentially normal MRI of the brain. Two punctate non-specific white matter foci in the right frontal periventricular region, which are of uncertain significance and may represent small vessel ischemic changes, migraine-related changes, or normal variants. No findings to suggest encephalitis, demyelination, mass lesion, or acute infarction. Importantly, the medial temporal lobes appear normal bilaterally.

differential_by_imaging.diagnosis: Normal variant / non-specific white matter changes
differential_by_imaging.rationale: Small punctate periventricular foci are commonly seen as incidental findings in young adults
Only two foci, no perivenular morphology, no enhancement, does not meet McDonald criteria

recommended_actions: No imaging follow-up needed for the white matter foci in isolation
Correlate with clinical presentation and laboratory workup

BMP.clinical_significance: Low-normal; warrants monitoring for developing SIADH

Inflammatory markers.clinical_significance: Within normal limits; does not exclude autoimmune or inflammatory process

Other.clinical_significance: Normal; no evidence of rhabdomyolysis or significant muscle injury
Normal; non-elevated prolactin does not reliably distinguish epileptic from non-epileptic events

labs.interpretation: Comprehensive metabolic and laboratory workup is essentially unremarkable. CBC, BMP, inflammatory markers, thyroid function, hepatic panel, and CK are all within normal limits. Urine drug screen is negative, effectively ruling out substance-induced psychosis. Sodium is low-normal at 136 mEq/L, which does not yet meet criteria for hyponatremia but warrants monitoring. The normal laboratory profile could be consistent with a primary psychiatric disorder or with early autoimmune encephalitis, which may present with entirely normal routine labs.

special_tests.Anti-NMDA receptor antibodies: Pending (send-out test, results expected in 7-10 days)

csf.interpretation: CSF is essentially normal with no pleocytosis, normal protein, and normal glucose ratio. Opening pressure is normal. Gram stain and bacterial culture are negative. Viral PCRs for HSV-1, HSV-2, VZV, and enterovirus are negative. Cytology shows no malignant cells. Anti-NMDA receptor antibodies and oligoclonal bands have been sent and are pending. The near-normal CSF does not exclude autoimmune encephalitis, as up to 20% of anti-NMDAR encephalitis cases may have normal initial CSF parameters, particularly early in the disease course.

NMDAR Antibody Panel.clinical_significance: Low-positive titer in serum; CSF testing is more sensitive and specific for diagnosis
Positive CSF anti-NMDA receptor antibodies confirm the diagnosis of anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis, even at a relatively low titer. Lower titers can be seen early in the disease course.

output.interpretation: Anti-NMDA receptor antibodies are positive in both serum (1:80) and CSF (1:64). Although titers are relatively low compared to advanced cases, the presence of CSF antibodies is diagnostic of anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis. Low titers early in the disease course are well-documented and do not exclude the diagnosis. Early detection and treatment are critical for improved outcomes. Repeat antibody titers can be used to monitor treatment response.

Pelvic Ultrasound.value: Normal, 3.0 x 2.2 x 2.1 cm with multiple small follicles. No masses or cystic lesions.
Normal, 2.7 x 1.8 x 1.9 cm with normal follicular pattern.
Normal size and echotexture, endometrial stripe 7 mm, consistent with proliferative phase.

output.interpretation: Normal pelvic ultrasound. No ovarian teratoma identified. While ovarian teratomas are associated with anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis in approximately 40-50% of young women, a normal pelvic ultrasound does not exclude the diagnosis. Approximately half of anti-NMDAR encephalitis cases in young women occur without an identifiable tumor. If clinical suspicion remains, MRI pelvis may provide additional sensitivity. Serial imaging may be considered over time as small teratomas can be occult on initial imaging.

Autoimmune Encephalitis Panel.clinical_significance: Low-positive ANA; non-specific finding, seen in up to 15% of healthy young women. Given family history of SLE, may warrant monitoring but does not alter the primary diagnosis of anti-NMDAR encephalitis.

output.interpretation: The autoimmune encephalitis antibody panel confirms anti-NMDA receptor antibody positivity in CSF. All other neuronal surface antibodies are negative. Anti-TPO is normal, excluding Hashimoto encephalopathy. A low-positive ANA (1:80, speckled) is noted, which is a non-specific finding frequently seen in healthy young women and does not indicate systemic lupus. The results confirm anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis as the primary diagnosis.

additional_observations: No ovarian masses or adnexal abnormalities
output.impression: Normal CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis. No occult malignancy, teratoma, or lymphadenopathy identified. In the setting of confirmed anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis without identifiable tumor, the case is classified as non-paraneoplastic. Serial surveillance imaging is recommended, as occult teratomas may become apparent on follow-up.
recommended_actions: Consider MRI pelvis for higher sensitivity in detecting small ovarian teratomas
Repeat pelvic imaging in 6 months and then annually for at least 2 years

special_tests.Oligoclonal bands: Positive (3 bands, not present in matched serum)
special_tests.Anti-NMDA receptor antibodies: Positive (titer 1:128, increased from 1:64 on initial LP)

output.interpretation: Repeat lumbar puncture now shows mild lymphocytic pleocytosis (18 cells/uL, 92% lymphocytes) and borderline elevated protein (48 mg/dL), representing interval change from the initially normal CSF. Oligoclonal bands are now present with a mildly elevated IgG index, indicating intrathecal antibody synthesis. The anti-NMDA receptor antibody titer has increased from 1:64 to 1:128, consistent with evolving autoimmune encephalitis. This evolution from initially normal CSF to inflammatory CSF is a well-recognized phenomenon in anti-NMDAR encephalitis and underscores the importance of repeat lumbar puncture when clinical suspicion is high despite initially normal results.

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: No sustained posterior dominant rhythm identifiable
background.overall_organization: Moderately disorganized, worse than initial EEG

findings.morphology: Semi-rhythmic, moderate to high amplitude (80-150 microvolts)
findings.state: Present during wakefulness and persisting into drowsiness
findings.clinical_correlation: More prominent than on initial EEG; reflects worsening diffuse cortical dysfunction

findings.frequency: 1-2 Hz delta with superimposed 20-25 Hz beta bursts
findings.morphology: Rhythmic delta waves with overlying fast activity developing in short bursts of 3-8 seconds
findings.state: Intermittent, seen during both wakefulness and sleep
findings.clinical_correlation: Extreme delta brush pattern emerging, highly characteristic of anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis. This pattern was not seen on the initial 30-minute EEG and has developed as the disease progresses.

findings.morphology: Rhythmic theta activity evolving to repetitive sharp waves over 45 seconds
findings.clinical_correlation: Subclinical seizure consistent with seizure burden in autoimmune encephalitis

artifacts.description: Intermittent movement artifact from patient restlessness and emerging orofacial movements

output.impression: Twenty-four-hour continuous EEG shows significant interval worsening compared to the initial 30-minute EEG. The background is now moderately disorganized with generalized rhythmic delta activity. An emerging extreme delta brush pattern is identified, which is highly characteristic of anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis. One subclinical electrographic seizure of right temporal origin was captured. These findings strongly support an evolving autoimmune encephalitis and should prompt urgent immunotherapy.

Psychiatric Consultation Note.value: Atypical psychotic presentation. While symptoms initially suggest first-break psychosis (paranoia, disorganized thinking, possible auditory hallucinations), several features are atypical: (1) very rapid onset over days rather than the gradual prodrome typical of primary psychosis, (2) patient has no prior psychiatric history beyond well-controlled anxiety, (3) subtle intermittent oral automatisms observed, (4) cognitive impairment (memory deficits, disorientation) disproportionate to the level of psychosis, (5) family history of autoimmune disease. Recommend medical workup before attributing to primary psychiatric disorder.
Psychiatric Consultation Note.clinical_significance: Psychiatric consultation raises concern for organic etiology rather than primary psychiatric disorder

Psychiatric Consultation Note.value: Defer antipsychotic initiation pending autoimmune and infectious workup. Low-dose benzodiazepines preferred for acute agitation management. If autoimmune encephalitis is excluded and primary psychosis confirmed, recommend starting low-dose second-generation antipsychotic.
Psychiatric Consultation Note.clinical_significance: Avoiding antipsychotics in possible anti-NMDAR encephalitis reduces risk of neuroleptic sensitivity

output.interpretation: Psychiatry consultation identifies several red flags for an organic rather than primary psychiatric etiology: rapid onset, prominent cognitive impairment, subtle movement abnormalities, and family autoimmune history. The consultant recommends holding antipsychotics and completing a thorough medical workup including autoimmune encephalitis antibody panel before establishing a psychiatric diagnosis.
abnormal_values_summary: Atypical psychotic presentation with red flags for organic etiology

differential.diagnosis: Primary psychiatric disorder (first-break psychosis / brief psychotic disorder)
differential.key_distinguishing: Most common initial misdiagnosis. However, the rapid onset over days, prominent cognitive impairment beyond what is typical for first-break psychosis, subtle oral automatisms, recent viral prodrome, and family autoimmune history are red flags for an organic process. Normal EEG and MRI do not exclude autoimmune encephalitis.

HSV encephalitis typically presents with high fevers, more acute confusion, and temporal lobe MRI abnormalities. This patient has near-normal temperature, normal MRI, and negative HSV PCR. However, early HSV can have a similar presentation before MRI changes develop.

Can present with psychiatric symptoms and encephalopathy. However, requires markedly elevated anti-TPO antibodies, and this patient has normal thyroid function and normal anti-TPO. Family autoimmune history is a red herring in this context.

College-age patient with potential access to substances. However, urine drug screen is negative, blood alcohol undetectable, and the progressive course over a week argues against an acute substance-related episode.

differential.diagnosis: Systemic lupus erythematosus with CNS involvement (neuropsychiatric lupus)
differential.key_distinguishing: Family history of SLE may raise suspicion. However, normal inflammatory markers, lack of systemic features (rash, arthritis, serositis, cytopenias), and ultimately the identification of anti-NMDAR antibodies distinguish this from neuropsychiatric lupus. A low-positive ANA is a non-specific finding.

optimal_actions.action: Ensure patient safety: place on 1:1 observation, establish IV access, continuous telemetry monitoring. Use low-dose benzodiazepines (lorazepam 1-2 mg IV/IM) for acute agitation rather than antipsychotics.
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Patient stabilization without masking neurological symptoms

optimal_actions.action: Obtain comprehensive labs: CBC, BMP, CRP, ESR, CK, liver function, thyroid function (TSH, free T4), urine drug screen, blood alcohol, prolactin
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Largely normal results; negative drug screen rules out substance-induced psychosis; normal thyroid argues against Hashimoto encephalopathy

optimal_actions.action: Order brain MRI with and without contrast
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal or near-normal MRI; recognizing that normal MRI does NOT exclude anti-NMDAR encephalitis (50% of cases have normal MRI)

optimal_actions.action: Perform lumbar puncture for CSF analysis including cell count, protein, glucose, Gram stain, cultures, HSV PCR, other viral PCRs, oligoclonal bands, and anti-NMDA receptor antibodies
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Initially near-normal CSF; critical to send anti-NMDAR antibodies even with normal routine CSF parameters

optimal_actions.action: Obtain EEG; if initial routine EEG is non-specific, escalate to continuous EEG monitoring given clinical suspicion for encephalitis
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Initial EEG shows mild non-specific slowing; continuous EEG may later reveal emerging extreme delta brush or subclinical seizures

optimal_actions.action: Request psychiatry consultation but explicitly communicate concern for organic etiology and request that antipsychotics be deferred pending autoimmune workup
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Psychiatry identifies atypical features and agrees to defer antipsychotic initiation

optimal_actions.action: Start empiric IV acyclovir 10 mg/kg q8h while awaiting HSV PCR results
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Empiric coverage until HSV encephalitis can be reliably excluded

optimal_actions.action: When anti-NMDAR antibodies return positive: immediately initiate first-line immunotherapy with IV methylprednisolone 1 g daily x 5 days AND IVIG 0.4 g/kg/day x 5 days
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Initiation of immunotherapy to halt antibody-mediated neuronal damage

optimal_actions.action: Order pelvic ultrasound to screen for ovarian teratoma
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal pelvic ultrasound in this case; no teratoma identified, but does not change the diagnosis

optimal_actions.action: If clinical deterioration occurs before antibody results return, consider empiric immunotherapy based on clinical suspicion
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Early immunotherapy may prevent progression to seizures, autonomic instability, and ICU admission

optimal_actions.action: Order CT chest/abdomen/pelvis for comprehensive paraneoplastic tumor screening
If insufficient improvement after first-line immunotherapy: escalate to second-line therapy with rituximab 375 mg/m2 weekly x 4 doses

critical_actions: Perform lumbar puncture and send CSF for anti-NMDA receptor antibodies, even when routine CSF parameters are normal
Do not attribute the presentation to primary psychiatric illness without excluding autoimmune encephalitis
Start empiric acyclovir until HSV PCR returns negative
Defer antipsychotic medications pending autoimmune workup, as they can worsen anti-NMDAR encephalitis and mask evolving symptoms
Initiate immunotherapy promptly once diagnosis is confirmed or strongly suspected
Screen for ovarian teratoma with pelvic imaging
Consider continuous EEG monitoring when routine EEG is non-specifically abnormal and clinical suspicion for encephalitis remains
contraindicated_actions: Diagnosing as primary psychiatric disorder and discharging without autoimmune encephalitis workup
Administering high-dose typical antipsychotics (haloperidol), which can cause severe rigidity and neuroleptic sensitivity in anti-NMDAR encephalitis patients
Dismissing the case because the initial MRI and CSF are normal — both can be normal early in anti-NMDAR encephalitis
Delaying lumbar puncture because the presentation 'looks psychiatric'
Attributing the viral prodrome to coincidence without considering it as a potential trigger for autoimmune encephalitis
Performing brain biopsy when antibody testing has not been completed
key_reasoning_points: The most critical diagnostic challenge in this case is recognizing that a presentation appearing overwhelmingly psychiatric (paranoia, disorganized behavior, insomnia in a young woman with psychosocial stressors) may represent early anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis
Normal initial MRI, near-normal CSF, and non-specific EEG are common early in anti-NMDAR encephalitis and should not be used to exclude the diagnosis

Red flags for organic psychosis include: very rapid onset (days vs weeks-months for primary psychosis), prominent cognitive impairment (disorientation, memory deficits), subtle oral automatisms, recent viral prodrome, and family history of autoimmune disease
Anti-NMDAR antibodies may take 7-10 days to result; the clinician must maintain suspicion and consider empiric immunotherapy if the patient deteriorates while waiting
The absence of an ovarian teratoma does not exclude anti-NMDAR encephalitis; approximately 50% of cases in young women occur without an identifiable tumor
Repeat lumbar puncture may show evolving abnormalities (pleocytosis, oligoclonal bands) even when the initial LP was normal, supporting the importance of serial assessment
A family history of autoimmune disease (SLE, Graves disease) is a risk factor for autoimmune encephalitis and should heighten clinical suspicion
The characteristic extreme delta brush EEG pattern may not appear until later in the disease course; an initially non-specific EEG does not rule out the diagnosis

metadata.author: NeuroBench Generator
metadata.diagnostic_challenge: Presentation mimics first-break psychosis with normal initial workup; requires high index of suspicion for autoimmune etiology
